content.subtitle: One situation, different choices
content.description: The same future event can often be expressed with more than one structure. The form you choose depends on meaning, speaker intention, and style.
examples: The seminar will start at 10 tomorrow.
The seminar is starting at 10 tomorrow.
The seminar is going to start at 10 tomorrow.

content.subtitle: What affects your choice?
items: Function: prediction, offer, decision, request, promise, refusal, arrangement, timetable, etc.
How fixed the event feels: planned, scheduled, uncertain, or based on present evidence.
Formality: **will** is often more formal than **be going to** in many contexts.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Predictions
Future facts
Decisions made at the moment of speaking
Offers
Promises
Requests
Refusals (won't)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I think travel costs will rise again next year.
The results will be published on Monday.
That phone is ringing — I'll get it.
I'll carry those boxes for you.
I won't share your draft with anyone.
Will you check this final paragraph for me?
No, I won't agree to that deadline.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: With **I/we** in offers and suggestions, **shall** is common: **Shall I open the window? / Shall we leave now?**
For requests, **would/could** are usually more polite than **will/can**.
For refusals, **couldn't/can't** may be softer than **won't** in some contexts.

content.subtitle: British usage note
items: In British English, **shall** is sometimes used with **I/we** instead of **will** in formal style.
Example: **I shall contact you once the review is complete.**

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Predictions based on present evidence
Plans or intentions already in mind

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Look at those black clouds — it's going to storm.
I'm going to apply for a master's programme next autumn.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Present continuous: personal arrangements
Present simple: timetables, programmes, fixed schedules
Present continuous is less common for very distant or uncertain plans

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I'm meeting the admissions tutor on Thursday afternoon.
We're flying to Dublin next weekend.
The train leaves at 06:45.
The conference opens on 14 June.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: For distant ambitions, **be going to** is often more natural than present continuous.
Compare: **I'm going to work abroad one day** (more natural) vs **I'm working abroad one day** (less natural).

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Future perfect simple: completed action before a future time
Future perfect simple: completed states/situations by a deadline
Future perfect continuous: activity continuing up to a future time (focus on duration)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: By 8 p.m., they will have finished the final edit.
By next July, she will have worked here for ten years.
By next July, she will have been working here for ten years.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Action in progress at a specific future time
Expected event in the normal course of things
Repeated or characteristic behaviour around a future period

content.subtitle: Examples
items: This time tomorrow I'll be presenting our findings.
Professor Khan will be joining us after lunch.
In a few years, more teams will be collaborating entirely online.

content.subtitle: Core rule
content.description: After time linkers such as **when, before, after, once, as soon as, until**, we normally use present forms in the time clause (present simple, present continuous, present perfect simple/continuous as needed), not **will** or **be going to**.
examples: I'll call you when I arrive.
We'll decide while we're reviewing the alternatives.
She'll apply once she's completed the portfolio.
They won't confirm anything until they've reviewed all the evidence.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Use **will** in the main clause if needed, but normally not inside the time clause itself.
Correct: **I'll text you when I get home.**
Incorrect pattern: **I'll text you when I'll get home.**

sections.title: Other Ways to Express the Future

useItems: very near future, often dramatic context
examples: She is on the verge of announcing the result.
The panel is due to publish its report tomorrow.
All candidates are to remain in the hall until 12:00.
They might delay the launch if funding is reduced.

content.subtitle: Backshifting future forms
content.description: When reporting a future idea from a past viewpoint, forms usually shift back: **will → would**, **is going to → was going to**, present forms for scheduled future events often shift to past forms.
examples: Then: I think the committee will approve it. → Later: I thought the committee would approve it.
Then: We're going to present on Friday. → Later: We were going to present on Friday.
Then: The bus leaves at six. → Later: She said the bus left at six.

sections.title: A: Circle the correct word or phrase (will vs. be going to vs. present continuous)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Marco looks like he's **falling / going to fall** asleep in the lecture any second!
When I leave school, I'm **being / going to be** a marine biologist.
Once Nina's finished her thesis, she's **moving / going to move** to Edinburgh for a year.
Slow down or you're **losing / going to lose** control of the bicycle!
When are they **recognising / going to recognise** that they need to update their environmental policy?
When are you **opening / going to open** a second campus in the city centre?
It sounds like we're not having **/ going to have** a graduation ceremony this year.
I think ferry prices are **rising / going to rise** again in about six months.
Why is Daniel **applying / going to apply** for that grant? He doesn't meet the criteria at all.
We're **covering / going to cover** this topic in more depth in the next seminar.
I'm fairly certain that old warehouse is **being / going to be** converted into student flats next month.
There's no doubt the discoveries of the past decade are **being / going to be** studied by future scientists.
You're not **withdrawing / going to withdraw** your application tomorrow, are you?
Lucas and Sofia aren't **getting / going to get** too comfortable when they publish their results, are they?
The renewable energy sector is **having / going to have** to deal with serious supply issues in the near future.

sections.title: B: Circle the correct word or phrase (will vs. present continuous vs. shall)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: I can't come to the festival this weekend, I'm afraid, as **we'll visit / we're visiting** my cousin in Bristol.
The Director **will address / is addressing** all of your concerns at the next assembly.
What time **does your train arrive / is your train arriving**?
items.answer: does your train arrive / is your train arriving

items.sentence: **I'll babysit / I'm babysitting** for the neighbours tomorrow evening so I can't come to the cinema.
**Shall / Will** we move on to the next point on the agenda now?
'She doesn't look well at all.' '**Is she going to / Will she be** sick?'
items.answer: Is she going to / Will she be

items.sentence: The last coach **leaves / is leaving** at half past eleven.
Elena, **shall / will** I give you a hand with that essay?
I'm afraid **I'm not having / I won't have** time to review all of this before the deadline.
When **does she / is she going to** get round to renewing her student visa?

sections.title: C: Write a short phrase (will, be going to, present simple, present continuous)
sections.instructions: Write a short phrase using will, be going to, present simple or present continuous on the line. If more than one form is correct, write all possibilities.

items.sentence: (I / come) …………… to the library on Sunday afternoon if you like, Sam.
Priya, (you / show) …………… Felix how to use the laboratory equipment, please?
items.answer: will you show / could you show

items.sentence: From the look of things, (it / rain) …………… heavily this afternoon.
Hurry up! (We / miss) …………… the last train!
items.answer: We're going to miss / We'll miss

items.sentence: (The results / announce) …………… on 22nd February.
items.answer: The results are announced /The results will be announced /The results are going to be announced

items.sentence: I can't come to the seminar this morning because (I / go) …………… to the health centre.
Don't worry! (They / cancel) …………… any courses for at least another semester.
items.answer: They aren't going to cancel / They won't cancel

items.sentence: (Miguel / get) …………… a place on the programme when he applies?
items.answer: Will Miguel get / Is Miguel going to get

items.sentence: No, I'm afraid (I / be) …………… at the conference tomorrow.
(The new app / release) …………… in the autumn.
items.answer: The new app launches /The new app is launching /The new app will launch /The new app is going to launch

items.sentence: (I / book) …………… the accommodation, shall I?
It looks like (several positions / cut) …………… in the next round of changes.
items.answer: several positions are going to be cut

items.sentence: What time (the coach / depart) …………… on Saturday?

sections.title: D: Future Perfect (correct or incorrect)
sections.instructions: If the phrase in bold is correct, put OK. If it is incorrect, write the correct form on the line so that the sentence is in the future perfect.

items.sentence: I think **we'll have sold out** of these tickets by the end of the afternoon.
Next month, Rosa **will have been being** with the research team for exactly four years.
**We'll have been becoming** the leading institute in the field by the end of the year.
At the end of next semester, **I'll have taking** the same route to campus every morning for two years.
When we meet on Friday, **will you already have** a chance to look at the draft report?
**You'll have driven** for hours without a break when we reach the conference venue, so you'll need to rest.
A new department head **will have appointed** by the end of the month.
Gabriela **won't have been finished** the assignment by then, but she should have made a start.
He definitely **will have been expecting** the announcement.
**Will you have managed** to write up your notes by the end of the day?
Why **will they have been waiting** for so long? I thought you'd told them the seminar had been postponed.
It sounds like the invitations **won't all have delivering** by next Thursday.

sections.title: E: Future Perfect Simple / Continuous / Future Continuous
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form (future perfect simple, future perfect continuous or future continuous). If more than one form is correct, write all possibilities.

items.sentence: By the end of today …………… (Lena / submit) over twenty job applications!
This time next year …………… (I / hopefully / run) my own environmental consultancy.
In four months' time …………… (we / live) in this city for exactly three years.
items.answer: we will have been living / will have lived

items.sentence: …………… (Oscar / already / arrive) by the time we reach the venue?
…………… (you / travel) all day, will you? Surely …………… (you / have) a few stopovers on the way, won't you?
items.answer: Will you be travelling, you will have

items.sentence: …………… (we / wait) for you outside the main entrance when you get there.
At the end of this fundraising marathon, …………… (500 volunteers / pack) food parcels non-stop for 24 hours!
…………… (the scientists / collect) data all morning, so they'll need a short break before the afternoon briefing.
items.answer: The scientists will have been collecting

items.sentence: How many candidates …………… (you / assess) for the scholarship tomorrow?
items.answer: will you be assessing / will you assess

items.sentence: …………… (Petra / read) the briefing notes as she's only just arrived, so we'd better go over the key points first.
What …………… (you / do) this time next month?
…………… (we / see) Ivan and Yuki when we visit Prague as they'll be travelling at the time, unfortunately.

sections.title: F: Time Clauses (similar meaning)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: You can all evacuate the building when ......
items.context: The alarm will sound and then you can all evacuate the building.

items.sentence: I'll keep a record while ......
items.context: Beatriz will ask the questions and I'll keep a record.

items.sentence: You become a full member of staff once ......
items.answer: you have been on probation for six months
items.context: You're on probation for six months and then you become a full member of staff.

items.sentence: Ms Torres will be presenting the data while ......
items.context: Ms Torres will be presenting the data and Mr Fenn will be recording the session.

items.sentence: We'll reassess the situation as ......
items.answer: soon as the test results come through
items.context: The test results will come through and then we'll reassess the situation.

items.sentence: We'll only consider offering you a paid role once ......
items.answer: you have volunteered with us for over a year
items.context: Volunteer with us for over a year and we'll consider offering you a paid role.

items.sentence: They won't expand until ......
items.context: They'll attract enough investment and then they'll expand.

items.sentence: I'll know for sure that the book is going to be published when I ......
items.answer: have been offered a contract by the publisher
items.context: The publisher will offer me a contract and then I'll know for sure the book is coming out.

sections.title: G: Information for conference participants (time clauses)
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: When you (1) ...... at the main entrance, show the security staff your delegate badge.
You (2) ...... where to collect your conference pack and how to reach the main hall.
As soon as you (3) ...... your name to the volunteer at the desk, you (4) ...... to your assigned seating area.
While you (5) ...... in the reception area, please complete the online registration form.
When you (6) ...... to speak, please make your way to the podium.
Your presentation slot (7) ...... for approximately 20 minutes.
Please note that all sessions (8) ...... .
Once the conference programme (9) ...... over, all recordings (10) ...... on the event website.
You (11) ...... by email as soon as the proceedings document (12) ...... .
items.answer: will be notified, has been compiled

sections.title: H: Sentence transformation (future expressions)
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. Use between three and eight words.

items.sentence: Nadia ...... (yet)
items.answer: has yet to find out if she has been accepted
items.context: Nadia will find out if she has been accepted onto the course.

items.sentence: They ...... (point)
items.answer: are on the point of deciding whether to relocate the headquarters
items.context: They will decide very soon whether to relocate the headquarters.

items.answer: am due to deliver a report to the committee on Thursday afternoon
items.context: The plan is that I will deliver a report to the committee on Thursday afternoon.

items.sentence: Are you ...... (about)
items.context: Are you leaving the building in a moment?

items.sentence: Actually, we ...... (verge)
items.answer: are not on the verge of a public announcement
items.context: Actually, there won't be a public announcement for a while.

items.sentence: What time ...... (due)
items.answer: are the external examiners due to arrive tomorrow
items.context: What time do the external examiners arrive tomorrow?

items.sentence: You are ...... (to)
items.answer: not to share this with anyone / to keep this confidential

items.sentence: The university ...... (introducing)
items.answer: is to introduce a new grading system/ is going to introduce a new grading system
items.context: The university has decided to introduce a new grading system.

sections.title: I: Future in the past (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The research team ………………… going to publish their findings last spring, but the peer review process caused a delay.
Nobody could have guessed that Martina ………………… announce her retirement so suddenly.
We were all gathered around the screen, knowing that the scientist ………………… to reveal the experiment's outcome within the next few minutes.
Ben ………………… to finish the report quickly, or his supervisor would discover he had missed the original deadline.
………………… you going to apply for the position before you heard it had already been filled internally?
………………… Ana ………………… on a field trip the following week anyway, or did she have to arrange everything at the last moment?
You're lucky you reached me in time. I ………………… just on the point of boarding the train.
We ………………… due to present at three, so I left a message saying we ………………… be there by five instead.
I think they ………………… about to cancel my contract when I resigned!
Carla understood the coded note. She was ………………… make contact with her colleagues on the next available flight.
We are delighted to confirm that Dr Reyes ...... take over as Head of Department from 1st October.
I ...... Hana for her advice on the proposal, but I'm not entirely sure that's the right move now.
Once ...... renewable energy, most countries won't want to return to fossil fuels.
...... look into the costs before we reach a final decision?
...... to the Dean or to the Faculty Board?
options: B Will you have been reporting
D Are you on the verge of reporting

items.sentence: This time next week, Sofia will ...... across the Atlantic for exactly four months.
When ...... giving me proper feedback on my work?
options: B are they going to start

items.sentence: Were you really just on the point ...... ?
